syllabus.content: States of matter, atoms, elements and compounds, stoichiometry, electrochemistry, chemical energetics, chemical reactions, acids, bases and salts, the Periodic Table, metals, chemistry of the environment, organic chemistry, experimental techniques and chemical analysis

subtopics: 1.1 Solids, liquids and gases: Molecular structures and property comparisons
1.2 Diffusion: Impact of relative molecular mass and temperature on gas particles
2.1 Atomic structure and the Periodic Table: Protons, neutrons, electrons, and isotopes
2.2 Ions and ionic bonds: Formation of positive and negative ions
2.3 Simple molecules and covalent bonds: Dot-and-cross diagrams and multiple bonds
2.4 Giant covalent structures: Properties of graphite, diamond, and silicon(IV) oxide
2.5 Metallic bonding: Lattice of positive ions and 'sea' of delocalised electrons
3.1 Formulae: Deducing formulae from compound names
3.2 Relative masses of atoms and molecules: Ar and Mr calculations
3.3 The mole and the Avogadro constant: Using L = 6.02 x 10^23
3.4 Amounts of substance: Reacting masses, gas volumes (24dm^3 at r.t.p), and concentrations
3.5 Percentage composition, yield and purity: Practical calculation in chemistry
6.1 Physical and chemical changes: Reversibility and identification
6.2 Rate of reaction: Collision theory (concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts)
6.3 Reversible reactions and equilibrium: Le Chatelier’s principle (pressure, temperature, and concentration)
6.4 Redox: Oxidation and reduction in terms of oxygen gain/loss and electron transfer
7.1 The characteristic properties of acids and bases: pH scale and indicators
7.2 Preparation of salts: Methods including titration and use of excess insoluble base
7.3 Identification of ions and gases: Qualitative analysis using flame tests and chemical reagents
11.1 Formulae, functional groups and terminology: Homologous series
11.2 Alkanes: Substitution reactions with chlorine
11.3 Alkenes: Addition reactions and catalytic cracking
11.4 Alcohols: Ethanol production via fermentation and catalytic addition of steam
11.5 Carboxylic acids: Reactions of ethanoic acid
11.6 Polymers: Addition and condensation polymerisation (polyesters and polyamides)

paper2.name: Multiple Choice (Extended)
paper2.details: 40 multiple-choice questions based on the Extended syllabus content [cite: 5]

paper4.name: Theory (Extended)
paper4.details: Short-answer and structured questions; requires knowledge of both Core and Supplement content [cite: 5]

paper6.name: Alternative to Practical
paper6.details: Tests experimental skills and qualitative analysis in a written format [cite: 5]

commandWords.define: Give precise meaning of a technical term [cite: 9]
commandWords.describe: State the points of a topic; give characteristics and main features [cite: 9]
commandWords.explain: Give reasons or make relationships between things evident [cite: 9]
commandWords.calculate: Work out from given facts, figures or information [cite: 9]
commandWords.deduce: Draw a conclusion from the information provided [cite: 9]
commandWords.predict: Suggest what may happen based on available information [cite: 9]

commonMistakes: Missing state symbols (s, l, g, aq) when explicitly requested in equations [cite: 1, 10]
Incorrect spelling of chemical names (e.g., confusing 'chlorine' with 'chloride')
Failure to show clear working steps in multi-mark stoichiometry calculations [cite: 11]
Drawing covalent dot-and-cross diagrams without showing the inner electron shells if requested
Inaccurate gas test results (e.g., stating hydrogen 'extinguishes' a splint rather than 'pops') [cite: 8]
examinerTips: Use the provided Periodic Table to verify atomic numbers and relative atomic masses [cite: 12]
Ensure chemical equations are always balanced, even if not specifically asked [cite: 10]
In qualitative analysis, specify the colour change (e.g., 'purple to colourless' for potassium manganate(VII)) rather than just 'decolourises' [cite: 8]
For equilibrium questions, always link the shift in position to the specific reason (e.g., 'fewer moles of gas on the left') [cite: 11]
In Paper 6, use precise apparatus names like 'volumetric pipette' or 'burette' for accurate measurement [cite: 12]
